chunks.text: The methods of fraud are becoming more sophisticated year by year.
Phone calls, emails, social media.
Criminals cleverly target gaps in the human heart.
Especially the " it's me, it's me " fraud, which targets the elderly
has been a social issue for years.
The caller pretends to be a relative and claims things like " I caused a traffic accident " or " I took money from the company.
The victim, shaken, loses normal reaction.
Told " please give the money by delivery right now," they do as instructed.
Sometimes the caller pretends to be a police officer or a bank agent.
They warn " your account has been used fraudulently " and try to obtain your password or verbal authentication.
Even if told " your family is in trouble," first become calm.
Immediately call the family member directly to confirm.
If the phone number is not identical, be suspicious.
Banks and government offices never ask for personally sensitive information by phone.
If you are asked, it is obviously a fraud.
If you are a victim, some people feel shame and cannot talk to anyone.
But it is important not to carry it alone.
Police hotlines and the establishment of consumer centers will respond kindly.
Fraudsters exploit human kindness and goodwill.
The feeling " for my family " is precious.
But there is no need to suppress that feeling.
With sensible judgment and action, you can protect both yourself and your family.
